content.text:
Memory stage: ActionItemUpdater v3.
        Current time: <instant>
        Timezone: Asia/Jerusalem
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:42ec0761

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, ACTION_ITEM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: Forced memory write; transcript includes durable facts about the user's backup choice/status and a remembered follow-up to buy a screwdriver, along with contextual notes from the backup setup process.

        Retrieval plan context:
        entity_queries=user|Google Drive|Google Drive backup|small screwdriver|laptop RAM upgrade|electronics store
        text_queries=Google Drive backup 100 GB plan working files|automatic backups Google Drive versioning backup status notifications|small screwdriver laptop RAM upgrade electronics store pick up
        memory_types=PROFILE, CLAIM, NOTE, ACTION_ITEM, SOURCE, ENTITY

        Stage instructions:
        You are ActionItemUpdater v3 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Create or update internal memory action items from explicit user commitments, deadlines, follow-ups, blockers, or action item lifecycle changes in TARGET_MESSAGE.

Definition:
An action item is Gromozeka's internal reminder/work-to-do memory for something the assistant or user should track later.
It is not the same thing as an external issue tracker item such as Jira Story, GitHub Issue, ticket, backlog item, or assignee field unless TARGET_MESSAGE explicitly asks Gromozeka to remember or track it as a follow-up.

Return JSON:
{
  "operations": [
    {
      "action": "insert | update | close | cancel | noop",
      "target_action_item_id": "existing-action-item-id-or-null",
      "action_item": {
        "title": "short English action title",
        "description": "English self-contained action item description or null",
        "status": "open | in_progress | blocked | done | cancelled",
        "priority": "low | normal | high",
        "due_at": "ISO-8601 or null",
        "scope_json": {
          "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
          "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
          "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
        },
        "owner_entity_id": "resolved-entity-id-or-null",
        "assignee_entity_id": "resolved-entity-id-or-null",
        "acceptance_criteria": ["..."],
        "blockers": ["..."],
        "related_entity_ids": ["resolved-entity-id"],
        "confidence": 0.0,
        "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE or null",
        "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
        "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the action item update"
      },
      "reason": "short explanation"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, description, scope text, acceptance criteria, blockers, evidence_reason, and reason in English.
- Create an action item only when TARGET_MESSAGE explicitly creates a future action, commitment, follow-up, deadline, blocker, assignment, or lifecycle update for Gromozeka memory.
- Do not convert external work records into action items just because they have Type=Story, Assignee, Status, Priority, or similar fields.
- A Jira Story, GitHub Issue, ticket, backlog item, or project-management row should usually become a source/note/claim about that external record, not an action item.
- A plain user question, request for an immediate chat answer, implementation instruction, discussion topic, preference, fact, rationale, or procedure is not automatically an action item.
- Do not create action items from "we should think about", weak ideas, or memory mechanics chatter unless the target explicitly says to keep a follow-up/action item/todo.
- Prefer one clear action item over several vague action items.
- Use Existing action items to update, close, cancel, or deduplicate instead of inserting duplicates.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE repeats an already open action item without new durable details, return noop targeting the existing action item.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE changes scope, acceptance criteria, priority, blockers, assignee, deadline, or wording of an existing action item, return update targeting that action item.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE says the action item is done, finished, completed, shipped, resolved, or no longer open because work was completed, return close.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE says the action item is cancelled, no longer needed, abandoned, dropped, or should not be done, return cancel.
- For close/cancel/update, target_action_item_id must be an existing action item id.
- Convert relative deadlines to absolute ISO-8601 when possible using Current time and Timezone.
- Use Full thread context to resolve pronouns and omitted subjects, but TARGET_MESSAGE is the only evidence for the action item update.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE when action item is not noop.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into owner_entity_id, assignee_entity_id, or related_entity_ids. If an action item is owned by the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE only asks the assistant to do something now in this chat, return noop unless it explicitly asks to remember/save a follow-up.
- Prefer noop over action item spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

        Existing action items:
        none

        Related memory context:
        none

        Resolved entities:
        - mention=I; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<0>; type=USER; name=User; reason=First-person statements require resolving the stable namespace-level user entity; no candidate exists.
- mention=Google Drive; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<1>; type=SERVICE; name=Google Drive; reason=Concrete reusable cloud backup service discussed as chosen by the user; no candidate exists.
- mention=my working files; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<2>; type=OTHER; name=User's working files; reason=Possessive wording identifies a reusable personal set of files that later claims may use as backup object.
- mention=my laptop; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<3>; type=PRODUCT; name=User's laptop; reason=Possessive wording identifies a concrete reusable personal device.
- mention=100 GB plan; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<4>; type=PRODUCT; name=Google Drive 100 GB plan; reason=Specific product/service plan selected by the user; no candidate exists.
- mention=the small screwdriver I use for that; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<5>; type=PRODUCT; name=User's small screwdriver for laptop RAM upgrades; reason=Possessive wording identifies a concrete personal tool, distinct from a replacement to be purchased.
- mention=a small screwdriver; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<6>; type=PRODUCT; name=Replacement small screwdriver for user's laptop RAM upgrade; reason=User needs to pick up a concrete replacement tool; useful as object for later pending-pickup claim.
- mention=my laptop's RAM; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<7>; type=PRODUCT; name=User's laptop RAM; reason=Concrete component of the user's laptop discussed as target of upgrade.
- mention=laptop RAM upgrade; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<8>; type=PROJECT; name=User's laptop RAM upgrade; reason=Explicit personal upgrade initiative likely to have durable related tasks and claims.
- mention=the electronics store near my place; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<9>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=Electronics store near user's place; reason=Specific local store the user plans to check for tools; no existing entity candidate.
- mention=my place; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<10>; type=LOCATION; name=User's place; reason=Possessive location used to identify the nearby electronics store; reusable for location claims.
- mention=current project; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<11>; type=PROJECT; name=Current project; reason=Default current-project subject for claims without a more specific resolved subject

LongMemEval past chat session.
Session date: 2023/08/11 (Fri) 19:15
Transcript:
user: I need help setting up automatic backups for my working files. Can you walk me through the process of setting up a backup system on my laptop?
assistant: Setting up a backup system is an essential step in protecting your important files from data loss due to hardware failure, software corruption, or accidental deletion. I'd be happy to guide you through the process of setting up a backup system on your laptop.

**Step 1: Identify Your Backup Needs**

Before we begin, let's determine what you need to back up:

* What type of files do you need to back up (e.g., documents, photos, videos, projects)?
* How often do you want to back up your files (e.g., daily, weekly, monthly)?
* Do you have any specific backup storage requirements (e.g., cloud storage, external hard drive, USB drive)?

**Step 2: Choose a Backup Method**

You have two primary options for backing up your files:

1. **Cloud Backup**: Store your files online using a cloud storage service like Google Drive, Dropbox, Microsoft OneDrive, or Backblaze. This method provides automatic backups, version history, and access to your files from anywhere.
2. **Local Backup**: Store your files on an external hard drive or USB drive connected to your laptop. This method provides a physical copy of your files, but you'll need to remember to back up your files regularly.

You can also consider a hybrid approach, where you use both cloud and local backups for added redundancy.

**Step 3: Select a Backup Software or Service**

Choose a backup software or service that fits your needs:

* **Cloud Backup Services**:
	+ Google Drive (includes Google Backup and Sync)
	+ Dropbox
	+ Microsoft OneDrive
	+ Backblaze
* **Local Backup Software**:
	+ Time Machine (for Mac)
	+ Backup and Restore (for Windows)
	+ Acronis True Image
	+ EaseUS Todo Backup

**Step 4: Set Up Your Backup**

Follow these general steps to set up your chosen backup method:

**Cloud Backup:**

1. Sign up for a cloud storage service and download the desktop app.
2. Install the app and sign in with your account credentials.
3. Select the folders and files you want to back up.
4. Choose your backup frequency (e.g., daily, weekly).
5. Set up any additional features, such as file versioning or encryption.

**Local Backup:**

1. Connect your external hard drive or USB drive to your laptop.
2. Install the backup software and follow the setup wizard.
3. Select the folders and files you want to back up.
4. Choose your backup frequency (e.g., daily, weekly).
5. Set up any additional features, such as scheduling or encryption.

**Step 5: Test Your Backup**

Test your backup system to ensure it's working correctly:

* Perform a manual backup to verify that your files are being backed up successfully.
* Check your backup storage to ensure your files are accessible and up-to-date.

**Step 6: Set Reminders and Automate Your Backups**

To ensure consistent backups, set reminders or automate your backups using the following methods:

* Cloud Backup: Set up recurring backups through the cloud service's settings.
* Local Backup: Use the backup software's scheduling feature to automate backups.

**Additional Tips:**

* Use the 3-2-1 rule: Have three copies of your files, store them on two different types of media, and keep one copy offsite (e.g., cloud storage).
* Consider using versioning, which allows you to restore previous versions of your files in case of data corruption or accidental changes.
* Keep your backup storage and laptop up-to-date with the latest software updates and security patches.

By following these steps, you'll have a reliable backup system in place to protect your important files. Remember to regularly test your backups and update your system to ensure your files remain safe.
user: I think I'll go with a cloud backup service, probably Google Drive since I already use it for other things. Can you tell me more about how to set up versioning in Google Drive, and do I need to upgrade my storage plan for that?
assistant: Google Drive is a popular and reliable cloud backup service. Versioning is a valuable feature that allows you to keep a history of changes to your files, so you can restore previous versions if needed. Here's how to set up versioning in Google Drive:

**Enabling Versioning in Google Drive:**

1. Sign in to your Google Drive account on the web or through the Google Drive desktop app.
2. Click on the gear icon in the top right corner and select "Settings."
3. Scroll down to the "Managing your account" section.
4. Click on "Version history and deleted files."
5. Toggle the switch next to "Keep revision history for" to the "ON" position.
6. Choose how long you want to keep file versions: 30 days, 60 days, or 100 revisions (the default is 30 days).

**Understanding Versioning in Google Drive:**

* Google Drive keeps a version history of your files for the selected period.
* Each time you edit a file, a new version is created and stored.
* You can view and restore previous versions of a file by right-clicking on the file and selecting "Manage versions."
* You can also use the "Revisions" tab in the file preview pane to view and restore previous versions.

**Storage Implications:**

* Versioning does consume additional storage space, as each version of a file is stored separately.
* The storage space used by versioning is calculated based on the total size of all file versions.
* If you're concerned about storage space, you can set a limit on the number of revisions kept (e.g., 100 revisions) or choose a shorter version history period (e.g., 30 days).

**Upgrading Your Storage Plan:**

* If you're currently using the free 15 GB storage plan, you might need to upgrade to a paid plan depending on the amount of data you plan to back up and the versioning settings you choose.
* Google Drive offers several paid plans, including:
	+ 100 GB: $1.99/month
	+ 200 GB: $3.99/month
	+ 1 TB: $9.99/month
	+ 2 TB: $19.99/month
	+ 10 TB: $99.99/month
* You can upgrade your storage plan at any time by going to the Google Drive settings and clicking on "Upgrade storage."

**Tips and Best Practices:**

* Regularly review your storage usage and adjust your versioning settings or storage plan as needed.
* Consider setting up a separate folder for your backed-up files to keep them organized and easy to manage.
* Use Google Drive's built-in features, such as file recovery and trash, to help manage your files and storage space.

By enabling versioning in Google Drive, you'll have an added layer of protection for your files and be able to recover previous versions if needed.
user: I think I'll go ahead with the 100 GB plan, that should be enough for my working files. By the way, I've been meaning to open up my laptop to upgrade the RAM, but I realized I misplaced the small screwdriver I use for that. I'll need to pick one up next time I'm out.
assistant: The 100 GB plan should provide ample storage space for your working files.

Regarding the RAM upgrade, I'm happy to help you with that too! Upgrading your laptop's RAM can definitely give it a performance boost. If you're comfortable with opening up your laptop and working with small components, it's a relatively straightforward process.

In the meantime, you can consider the following options to obtain a small screwdriver:

1. **Visit a local electronics or computer store**: Stop by a nearby electronics or computer store that sells laptop accessories or repair tools. They might carry a selection of small screwdrivers suitable for laptop maintenance.
2. **Online retailers**: You can also order a small screwdriver online from retailers like Amazon, Newegg, or eBay. Make sure to select a screwdriver with the correct type and size of tip (e.g., Torx, Phillips, or Pentalobe) required for your laptop model.
3. **Check your local hardware store**: If you have a local hardware store or a DIY center, they might carry a selection of small screwdrivers or be able to order one for you.

When you get the screwdriver, make sure to follow proper safety precautions when working with electronics, and consult your laptop's user manual or online resources for specific instructions on upgrading the RAM.

Feel free to ask if you have any questions or need further assistance with the RAM upgrade process!
user: I'll probably check out the electronics store near my place, they usually have a variety of tools and accessories. Anyway, back to the backup setup, can you walk me through the process of selecting which files and folders to back up on Google Drive?
assistant: The electronics store near your place should have the screwdriver you need.

Now, let's get back to setting up your Google Drive backup. Selecting the right files and folders to back up is crucial to ensure that your important data is protected. Here's a step-by-step guide to help you get started:

**Step 1: Identify Your Important Files and Folders**

Take a few minutes to think about which files and folders on your laptop are essential to you. These might include:

* Documents (e.g., Word, PDF, Text)
* Spreadsheets (e.g., Excel)
* Presentations (e.g., PowerPoint)
* Images (e.g., photos, graphics)
* Videos
* Projects (e.g., coding, design)
* Personal files (e.g., resumes, certificates)

**Step 2: Organize Your Files and Folders**

Organize your files and folders in a logical structure to make it easier to select what to back up. You can create folders for different categories, such as:

* Work
* Personal
* Projects
* Documents
* Media

**Step 3: Open Google Drive on Your Laptop**

Open the Google Drive desktop app on your laptop. If you haven't installed it yet, you can download it from the Google Drive website.

**Step 4: Select the Folders and Files to Back Up**

In the Google Drive app, click on the "My Drive" tab and then click on the "Add to My Drive" button. Select the folders and files you want to back up from your laptop. You can choose entire folders or individual files.

**Tips:**

* Start with the most important files and folders and then add more as needed.
* Consider backing up your desktop, documents, and downloads folders, as these often contain important files.
* If you have large files or folders, you might want to prioritize the most critical ones first.

**Step 5: Configure Backup Settings**

Once you've selected the folders and files, you can configure the backup settings. You can choose to:

* Back up files and folders continuously (recommended)
* Set a specific backup schedule (e.g., daily, weekly)
* Choose which file types to include or exclude from backups

**Step 6: Review and Confirm Your Backup Settings**

Review your backup settings to ensure you're backing up the right files and folders. Make any necessary changes before confirming your settings.

That's it! Your Google Drive backup should now be set up and running. Remember to monitor your storage usage and adjust your backup settings as needed.

If you have any questions or need further assistance, feel free to ask!
user: I think I've got everything set up for my Google Drive backup. One more thing, how do I know if my files are being backed up correctly? Is there a way to check the backup status or get notifications if something goes wrong?
assistant: Congratulations on setting up your Google Drive backup!

Yes, Google Drive provides several ways to monitor your backup status and receive notifications if something goes wrong. Here's how to check your backup status and set up notifications:

**Checking Backup Status:**

1. Open the Google Drive desktop app on your laptop.
2. Click on the "My Drive" tab.
3. Look for the "Backup" section on the right-hand side of the window.
4. Click on "View backup details" or "Backup status" to see the latest backup information.

You can also check the Google Drive website:

1. Sign in to your Google Drive account on the web.
2. Click on the gear icon in the top right corner.
3. Select "Settings."
4. Scroll down to the "Backup" section.
5. Click on "View backup details" or "Backup status."

**Backup Status Indicators:**

Google Drive displays different indicators to show the backup status:

* **Green checkmark**: Backup is complete and up-to-date.
* **Yellow exclamation mark**: Backup is in progress or paused.
* **Red exclamation mark**: Backup failed or encountered an error.

**Setting Up Notifications:**

To receive notifications if something goes wrong with your backup, follow these steps:

1. Sign in to your Google Drive account on the web.
2. Click on the gear icon in the top right corner.
3. Select "Settings."
4. Scroll down to the "Notifications" section.
5. Toggle the switch next to "Backup notifications" to the "ON" position.
6. Choose the types of notifications you want to receive (e.g., email, in-app notifications).

You can also set up notifications for specific events, such as:

* Backup failures
* Low storage space
* File conflicts

**Additional Tips:**

* Regularly check your backup status to ensure it's complete and up-to-date.
* If you encounter any issues, refer to Google Drive's support resources or contact their support team.
* Consider setting up two-factor authentication (2FA) to add an extra layer of security to your Google Drive account.

By monitoring your backup status and setting up notifications, you'll be able to identify and address any issues promptly, ensuring your files are safely backed up and protected.
user: I think I've got everything set up for my Google Drive backup now. Thanks for your help!
assistant: You're welcome! I'm glad I could assist you in setting up your Google Drive backup. It's great that you're taking proactive steps to protect your important files.

Remember to regularly check your backup status and storage usage to ensure everything is running smoothly. If you have any more questions or need further assistance, don't hesitate to ask.

Now, go ahead and enjoy the peace of mind that comes with having a reliable backup system in place!

(And don't forget to pick up that small screwdriver for your laptop's RAM upgrade when you're out)

responseFormat.description: Create or update internal memory action items from explicit commitments.
responseFormat.name: memory_action_item_updater

systemPrompts:
# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

        You are ActionItemUpdater v3 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Create or update internal memory action items from explicit user commitments, deadlines, follow-ups, blockers, or action item lifecycle changes in TARGET_MESSAGE.

Definition:
An action item is Gromozeka's internal reminder/work-to-do memory for something the assistant or user should track later.
It is not the same thing as an external issue tracker item such as Jira Story, GitHub Issue, ticket, backlog item, or assignee field unless TARGET_MESSAGE explicitly asks Gromozeka to remember or track it as a follow-up.

Return JSON:
{
  "operations": [
    {
      "action": "insert | update | close | cancel | noop",
      "target_action_item_id": "existing-action-item-id-or-null",
      "action_item": {
        "title": "short English action title",
        "description": "English self-contained action item description or null",
        "status": "open | in_progress | blocked | done | cancelled",
        "priority": "low | normal | high",
        "due_at": "ISO-8601 or null",
        "scope_json": {
          "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
          "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
          "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
        },
        "owner_entity_id": "resolved-entity-id-or-null",
        "assignee_entity_id": "resolved-entity-id-or-null",
        "acceptance_criteria": ["..."],
        "blockers": ["..."],
        "related_entity_ids": ["resolved-entity-id"],
        "confidence": 0.0,
        "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE or null",
        "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
        "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the action item update"
      },
      "reason": "short explanation"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, description, scope text, acceptance criteria, blockers, evidence_reason, and reason in English.
- Create an action item only when TARGET_MESSAGE explicitly creates a future action, commitment, follow-up, deadline, blocker, assignment, or lifecycle update for Gromozeka memory.
- Do not convert external work records into action items just because they have Type=Story, Assignee, Status, Priority, or similar fields.
- A Jira Story, GitHub Issue, ticket, backlog item, or project-management row should usually become a source/note/claim about that external record, not an action item.
- A plain user question, request for an immediate chat answer, implementation instruction, discussion topic, preference, fact, rationale, or procedure is not automatically an action item.
- Do not create action items from "we should think about", weak ideas, or memory mechanics chatter unless the target explicitly says to keep a follow-up/action item/todo.
- Prefer one clear action item over several vague action items.
- Use Existing action items to update, close, cancel, or deduplicate instead of inserting duplicates.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE repeats an already open action item without new durable details, return noop targeting the existing action item.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE changes scope, acceptance criteria, priority, blockers, assignee, deadline, or wording of an existing action item, return update targeting that action item.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE says the action item is done, finished, completed, shipped, resolved, or no longer open because work was completed, return close.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE says the action item is cancelled, no longer needed, abandoned, dropped, or should not be done, return cancel.
- For close/cancel/update, target_action_item_id must be an existing action item id.
- Convert relative deadlines to absolute ISO-8601 when possible using Current time and Timezone.
- Use Full thread context to resolve pronouns and omitted subjects, but TARGET_MESSAGE is the only evidence for the action item update.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE when action item is not noop.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into owner_entity_id, assignee_entity_id, or related_entity_ids. If an action item is owned by the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE only asks the assistant to do something now in this chat, return noop unless it explicitly asks to remember/save a follow-up.
- Prefer noop over action item spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

Transcript:
user: I need help setting up automatic backups for my working files. Can you walk me through the process of setting up a backup system on my laptop?
assistant: Setting up a backup system is an essential step in protecting your important files from data loss due to hardware failure, software corruption, or accidental deletion. I'd be happy to guide you through the process of setting up a backup system on your laptop.

**Step 1: Identify Your Backup Needs**

Before we begin, let's determine what you need to back up:

* What type of files do you need to back up (e.g., documents, photos, videos, projects)?
* How often do you want to back up your files (e.g., daily, weekly, monthly)?
* Do you have any specific backup storage requirements (e.g., cloud storage, external hard drive, USB drive)?

**Step 2: Choose a Backup Method**

You have two primary options for backing up your files:

1. **Cloud Backup**: Store your files online using a cloud storage service like Google Drive, Dropbox, Microsoft OneDrive, or Backblaze. This method provides automatic backups, version history, and access to your files from anywhere.
2. **Local Backup**: Store your files on an external hard drive or USB drive connected to your laptop. This method provides a physical copy of your files, but you'll need to remember to back up your files regularly.

You can also consider a hybrid approach, where you use both cloud and local backups for added redundancy.

**Step 3: Select a Backup Software or Service**

Choose a backup software or service that fits your needs:

* **Cloud Backup Services**:
	+ Google Drive (includes Google Backup and Sync)
	+ Dropbox
	+ Microsoft OneDrive
	+ Backblaze
* **Local Backup Software**:
	+ Time Machine (for Mac)
	+ Backup and Restore (for Windows)
	+ Acronis True Image
	+ EaseUS Todo Backup

**Step 4: Set Up Your Backup**

Follow these general steps to set up your chosen backup method:

**Cloud Backup:**

1. Sign up for a cloud storage service and download the desktop app.
2. Install the app and sign in with your account credentials.
3. Select the folders and files you want to back up.
4. Choose your backup frequency (e.g., daily, weekly).
5. Set up any additional features, such as file versioning or encryption.

**Local Backup:**

1. Connect your external hard drive or USB drive to your laptop.
2. Install the backup software and follow the setup wizard.
3. Select the folders and files you want to back up.
4. Choose your backup frequency (e.g., daily, weekly).
5. Set up any additional features, such as scheduling or encryption.

**Step 5: Test Your Backup**

Test your backup system to ensure it's working correctly:

* Perform a manual backup to verify that your files are being backed up successfully.
* Check your backup storage to ensure your files are accessible and up-to-date.

**Step 6: Set Reminders and Automate Your Backups**

To ensure consistent backups, set reminders or automate your backups using the following methods:

* Cloud Backup: Set up recurring backups through the cloud service's settings.
* Local Backup: Use the backup software's scheduling feature to automate backups.

**Additional Tips:**

* Use the 3-2-1 rule: Have three copies of your files, store them on two different types of media, and keep one copy offsite (e.g., cloud storage).
* Consider using versioning, which allows you to restore previous versions of your files in case of data corruption or accidental changes.
* Keep your backup storage and laptop up-to-date with the latest software updates and security patches.

By following these steps, you'll have a reliable backup system in place to protect your important files. Remember to regularly test your backups and update your system to ensure your files remain safe.
user: I think I'll go with a cloud backup service, probably Google Drive since I already use it for other things. Can you tell me more about how to set up versioning in Google Drive, and do I need to upgrade my storage plan for that?
assistant: Google Drive is a popular and reliable cloud backup service. Versioning is a valuable feature that allows you to keep a history of changes to your files, so you can restore previous versions if needed. Here's how to set up versioning in Google Drive:

**Enabling Versioning in Google Drive:**

1. Sign in to your Google Drive account on the web or through the Google Drive desktop app.
2. Click on the gear icon in the top right corner and select "Settings."
3. Scroll down to the "Managing your account" section.
4. Click on "Version history and deleted files."
5. Toggle the switch next to "Keep revision history for" to the "ON" position.
6. Choose how long you want to keep file versions: 30 days, 60 days, or 100 revisions (the default is 30 days).

**Understanding Versioning in Google Drive:**

* Google Drive keeps a version history of your files for the selected period.
* Each time you edit a file, a new version is created and stored.
* You can view and restore previous versions of a file by right-clicking on the file and selecting "Manage versions."
* You can also use the "Revisions" tab in the file preview pane to view and restore previous versions.

**Storage Implications:**

* Versioning does consume additional storage space, as each version of a file is stored separately.
* The storage space used by versioning is calculated based on the total size of all file versions.
* If you're concerned about storage space, you can set a limit on the number of revisions kept (e.g., 100 revisions) or choose a shorter version history period (e.g., 30 days).

**Upgrading Your Storage Plan:**

* If you're currently using the free 15 GB storage plan, you might need to upgrade to a paid plan depending on the amount of data you plan to back up and the versioning settings you choose.
* Google Drive offers several paid plans, including:
	+ 100 GB: $1.99/month
	+ 200 GB: $3.99/month
	+ 1 TB: $9.99/month
	+ 2 TB: $19.99/month
	+ 10 TB: $99.99/month
* You can upgrade your storage plan at any time by going to the Google Drive settings and clicking on "Upgrade storage."

**Tips and Best Practices:**

* Regularly review your storage usage and adjust your versioning settings or storage plan as needed.
* Consider setting up a separate folder for your backed-up files to keep them organized and easy to manage.
* Use Google Drive's built-in features, such as file recovery and trash, to help manage your files and storage space.

By enabling versioning in Google Drive, you'll have an added layer of protection for your files and be able to recover previous versions if needed.

In the meantime, you can consider the following options to obtain a small screwdriver:

When you get the screwdriver, make sure to follow proper safety precautions when working with electronics, and consult your laptop's user manual or online resources for specific instructions on upgrading the RAM.

Feel free to ask if you have any questions or need further assistance with the RAM upgrade process!
user: I'll probably check out the electronics store near my place, they usually have a variety of tools and accessories. Anyway, back to the backup setup, can you walk me through the process of selecting which files and folders to back up on Google Drive?
assistant: The electronics store near your place should have the screwdriver you need.

Now, let's get back to setting up your Google Drive backup. Selecting the right files and folders to back up is crucial to ensure that your important data is protected. Here's a step-by-step guide to help you get started:

**Step 1: Identify Your Important Files and Folders**

Take a few minutes to think about which files and folders on your laptop are essential to you. These might include:

* Documents (e.g., Word, PDF, Text)
* Spreadsheets (e.g., Excel)
* Presentations (e.g., PowerPoint)
* Images (e.g., photos, graphics)
* Videos
* Projects (e.g., coding, design)
* Personal files (e.g., resumes, certificates)

**Step 2: Organize Your Files and Folders**

Organize your files and folders in a logical structure to make it easier to select what to back up. You can create folders for different categories, such as:

* Work
* Personal
* Projects
* Documents
* Media

**Step 3: Open Google Drive on Your Laptop**

Open the Google Drive desktop app on your laptop. If you haven't installed it yet, you can download it from the Google Drive website.

**Step 4: Select the Folders and Files to Back Up**

In the Google Drive app, click on the "My Drive" tab and then click on the "Add to My Drive" button. Select the folders and files you want to back up from your laptop. You can choose entire folders or individual files.

**Tips:**

* Start with the most important files and folders and then add more as needed.
* Consider backing up your desktop, documents, and downloads folders, as these often contain important files.
* If you have large files or folders, you might want to prioritize the most critical ones first.

**Step 5: Configure Backup Settings**

Once you've selected the folders and files, you can configure the backup settings. You can choose to:

* Back up files and folders continuously (recommended)
* Set a specific backup schedule (e.g., daily, weekly)
* Choose which file types to include or exclude from backups

**Step 6: Review and Confirm Your Backup Settings**

Review your backup settings to ensure you're backing up the right files and folders. Make any necessary changes before confirming your settings.

That's it! Your Google Drive backup should now be set up and running. Remember to monitor your storage usage and adjust your backup settings as needed.

If you have any questions or need further assistance, feel free to ask!
user: I think I've got everything set up for my Google Drive backup. One more thing, how do I know if my files are being backed up correctly? Is there a way to check the backup status or get notifications if something goes wrong?
assistant: Congratulations on setting up your Google Drive backup!

Yes, Google Drive provides several ways to monitor your backup status and receive notifications if something goes wrong. Here's how to check your backup status and set up notifications:

**Checking Backup Status:**

1. Open the Google Drive desktop app on your laptop.
2. Click on the "My Drive" tab.
3. Look for the "Backup" section on the right-hand side of the window.
4. Click on "View backup details" or "Backup status" to see the latest backup information.

You can also check the Google Drive website:

1. Sign in to your Google Drive account on the web.
2. Click on the gear icon in the top right corner.
3. Select "Settings."
4. Scroll down to the "Backup" section.
5. Click on "View backup details" or "Backup status."

**Backup Status Indicators:**

Google Drive displays different indicators to show the backup status:

* **Green checkmark**: Backup is complete and up-to-date.
* **Yellow exclamation mark**: Backup is in progress or paused.
* **Red exclamation mark**: Backup failed or encountered an error.

**Setting Up Notifications:**

To receive notifications if something goes wrong with your backup, follow these steps:

1. Sign in to your Google Drive account on the web.
2. Click on the gear icon in the top right corner.
3. Select "Settings."
4. Scroll down to the "Notifications" section.
5. Toggle the switch next to "Backup notifications" to the "ON" position.
6. Choose the types of notifications you want to receive (e.g., email, in-app notifications).

You can also set up notifications for specific events, such as:

* Backup failures
* Low storage space
* File conflicts

**Additional Tips:**

* Regularly check your backup status to ensure it's complete and up-to-date.
* If you encounter any issues, refer to Google Drive's support resources or contact their support team.
* Consider setting up two-factor authentication (2FA) to add an extra layer of security to your Google Drive account.

By monitoring your backup status and setting up notifications, you'll be able to identify and address any issues promptly, ensuring your files are safely backed up and protected.
user: I think I've got everything set up for my Google Drive backup now. Thanks for your help!
assistant: You're welcome! I'm glad I could assist you in setting up your Google Drive backup. It's great that you're taking proactive steps to protect your important files.

Remember to regularly check your backup status and storage usage to ensure everything is running smoothly. If you have any more questions or need further assistance, don't hesitate to ask.

Now, go ahead and enjoy the peace of mind that comes with having a reliable backup system in place!

(And don't forget to pick up that small screwdriver for your laptop's RAM upgrade when you're out)
